coredata.dc:title: l-Histidine-protected copper nanoparticles as a fluorescent probe for sensing ferric ions
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Shu Min
Geng, Shuo
Li, Na
Liu, Shi Gang
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: Ferric ions (Fe3+) play an important role in various chemical and physiological functions. Thus, analytical methods for the detection of Fe3+ in the environment are highly in demand. In this work, a simple chemical reduction method for synthesizing water soluble l-histidine capped copper nanoparticles (l-His-CuNPs) was successfully established. l-Histidine and ascorbic acid were acted as the capping scaffold and reducing agent, respectively. The as-prepared l-His-CuNPs were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. The l-His-CuNPs show maximum luminescence at 495nm when excited at 385nm. Based on an aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (AIQ) mechanism, a sensitive and selective sensor for Fe3+ was developed. The detection limit (3s/slope) is 82nM and the linear range is from 0.10 to 20μM. Moreover, the developed sensor was successfully employed in the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Quenching
Copper nanoparticles
l-Histidine
Ferric ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration of l-His-CuNPs preparation and the principle of l-His-CuNPs for Fe3+ detection.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of l-His-CuNPs (5.4mgmL−1). The inset exhibits photographs of l-His-CuNPs under UV light (bottom) and visible light (top); (B) TEM image of l-His-CuNPs (pH 4.10).
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of l-His-CuNPs (5.4mgmL−1) under different excitations (pH 4.10). (B) FTIR spectra of pure l-histidine and l-His-CuNPs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of l-His-CuNPs detection system (solid line), in the presence of Fe3+ (dot line), and in the presence of Fe3+ and EDTA (dosh dot line). Conditions: volume of l-His-CuNPs, 490μL; concentration of l-His-CuNPs, 5.4mgmL−1; concentration of Fe3+, 20μM; reaction time: 60min; reaction temperature: 25°C; pH 4.10. Insets show photographs of l-His-CuNPs before (left) and after (right) adding 20μM Fe3+ under the UV light (bottom) and ambient light (top). (B) TEM image of l-His-CuNPs with 20μM Fe3+. (C) TEM image of l-His-CuNPs with 20μM Fe3+ and 40μM EDTA. (D) Absorption spectra of AA solution (a, 0.10M), Cu(NO3)2 solution (b, 0.10M), l-histidine solution (c, 0.10M) and l-His-CuNPs (5.4mgmL−1) in the presence (d) and absence (e) of 20μM Fe3+.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the l-His-CuNPs probe (5.4mgmL−1) to different amounts of Fe3+ at 385nm excitation. Fe3+ concentration (from a to m): 0, 0.1, 0.2, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 100, 200, 300 and 500μM. (B) Fluorescence intensity changes of l-His-CuNPs in the presence of increasing concentration of Fe3+, (Insets) Linear relationships of (F 0−F) vs concentration of Fe3+ from 0.1 to 20μM.
Fluorescence responses of the l-His-CuNPs probe (5.4mgmL−1) towards various metal ions (A) and anions (B), the concentrations of all ions are 100μM.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: NA-GQDs nitrogen-doped and amino acid functionalized graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: l-Histidine-protected copper nanoparticles as a fluorescent probe for sensing ferric ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferric ions (Fe3+) play an important role in various chemical and physiological functions. Thus, analytical methods for the detection of Fe3+ in the environment are highly in demand. In this work, a simple chemical reduction method for synthesizing water soluble l-histidine capped copper nanoparticles (l-His-CuNPs) was successfully established. l-Histidine and ascorbic acid were acted as the capping scaffold and reducing agent, respectively. The as-prepared l-His-CuNPs were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. The l-His-CuNPs show maximum luminescence at 495nm when excited at 385nm. Based on an aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (AIQ) mechanism, a sensitive and selective sensor for Fe3+ was developed. The detection limit (3s/slope) is 82nM and the linear range is from 0.10 to 20μM. Moreover, the developed sensor was successfully employed in the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal nanoparticles (NPs) have witnessed tremendous growth due to their interesting physical, optical, and electrical qualities in recent years [1,2]. Compared with gold, silver, and platinum NPs, the cost for preparation of copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) is obviously lower. In addition, CuNPs exhibit much more outstanding biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and environmental friendliness than semiconductor quantum dots. Therefore, CuNPs are increasingly significant in research materials and have been achieved great progress [3,4].

Recently, plenty of synthetic techniques for preparation of CuNPs have been developed, including chemical reduction [5], photochemical method [6], electrochemical method [7], thermal decomposition [8], laser ablation [9], biological methods [10], etc. Some researchers prepared CuNPs using different stabilizers such as starch, gum acacia, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, polyvinylpyrrolidone, gelatin, chitosan [11–16], etc. And the most widespread reducers are ascorbic acid (AA), sodium borohydride, poly (N-vinylpyrrolidone), hydrazinium hydroxide, amines or polyols [17–22]. The fluorescent CuNPs were usually used for the detection of small molecules, anions, metal ions, biomolecules, and so on [23–27].

Ferric ion (Fe3+) is a kind of essential trace elements in environmental and biological systems and plays a crucial role in chemical and physiological processes in organisms, such as enzymatic catalysis, electron transport, nucleic acid synthesis, and cellular metabolism [3,28]. As is well-known to us, the concentration of Fe3+ in drinking water is a key index to evaluate the quality of water in environmental monitoring and sewage treatment. Fe3+ can give water a reddish color, metallic taste, and turbidity, even at low concentrations, making the water unusable for beverage and food [29–31]. In addition, the presence of Fe3+ in biological systems numerously accumulates within the spleen, bone marrow cells, and liver. Fe3+ can provide an oxygen-carrying capacity of heme by binding to ferritin and plays a cofactor role in some enzymatic reactions [32,33]. The deficiency of Fe3+ would cause hemochromatosis, anemia, Parkinson’s disease, liver damage, cancer, and diabetes [34–40]. High levels of Fe3+ in organisms were connected with the increasing incidence of some dysfunction or cancers in liver, heart, pancreas, and other certain organs [41–45]. Thus, determination of Fe3+ is undoubtedly important in a water environment and health monitoring.

Many techniques for the detection of Fe3+ in different samples have been proposed, such as electrochemical inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [46], atomic absorption spectroscopy [47], inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [48], colorimetry [49], voltammetry [50], and fluorescence spectroscopy [51,52]. Among the various detection methods, fluorescence spectroscopic analysis for Fe3+ detection based on fluorescent probe has drawn increasing attention of researchers because of their unique properties and amusing characters. Recently, many fluorescent sensors for detecting Fe3+ have been developed through utilizing organic dye molecules, gold nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles, carbon nanoparticles, etc. [53–58]. However, nowadays, there are few studies on the fluorescent CuNP probes for Fe3+ sensing.

Herein, a simple chemosensor was developed to detect Fe3+. CuNPs stabilized by l-histidine (l-His) with blue-green fluorescence emission have been well prepared. Intriguingly, the l-His-CuNPs would aggregate into large particles when a certain concentration of Fe3+ exists, resulting in the quenching of the l-His-CuNPs fluorescence, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The concentration of Fe3+ can be detected with good sensitivity and selectivity. This method was applied to the detection of Fe3+ in tap water and Jialing River water with satisfactory results.

Typically, Cu(NO3)2 solution (4.5μL, 0.10M) was added to l-histidine solution (1.0mL, 0.10M) followed by the addition of AA solution (50.0μL, 0.10M) with magnetic stirring for 2min. After 24 hours' standing, the l-His-CuNPs were successfully synthesized. All experiments were conducted at ambient temperature (25°C). For long-term preservation, the l-His-CuNPs were freeze-dried under vacuum and stored in the refrigerator (–20°C). When the l-His-CuNPs aqueous solution was required, the solid samples could be dissolved with ultrapure water.

Briefly, 10μL different concentrations of Fe3+ were mixed with 100μL of 5.4mgmL−1 l-His-CuNPs solution. Then the above solution was diluted to 500μL with Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (10mM, pH 4.10), followed by thorough shaking and incubated at 25°C for 60min. Finally, the spectrofluorophotometer was used to record the fluorescence spectra and intensities (F 0 and F), where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of l-His-CuNPs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively.

Tap water and Jialing River water samples were collected from our laboratory and the Jialing River, respectively. The tap water samples were boiled to remove chlorine. The Jialing River water samples were centrifuged (10,000rpm) to get a supernatant, and filtered through a 0.45μm microfiltration membrane to remove the suspended particles.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of l-His-CuNPs was observed at 495nm when the excitation wavelength was at 385nm. The photographs shown in the inset of Fig. 2A indicated that the l-His-CuNPs exhibit a blue-green fluorescence (FL) under UV light (365nm) and are yellowish under ambient light. To confirm whether the l-His-CuNPs were successfully synthesized, the TEM image was obtained. It was found that the prepared l-His-CuNPs were well-dispersed and about 2nm in diameter (Fig. 2B).

Besides, when the excitation wavelength was changed from 305 to 405nm, no excitation-dependent characteristic was observed (Fig. 3 A). In a previous report, Yu et al. found that different sized Pt NCs in solution would induce the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior [59]. Thus, it was deduced that the prepared l-His-CuNPs are composed of one emitting and uniformly size species only. The above results indicated that the l-His-CuNPs were monodisperse, quite stable, and water soluble. Moreover, the synthesis process does not contain toxic reagents and illustrates that the obtained l-His-CuNPs may possess low toxicity and the synthesis method was simple. We also analyzed the FTIR spectra of l-His-CuNPs to investigate their surface structure. The come into being of the strong absorption band at 1633cm−1 is a result of the deprotonated carboxylic group generated the antisymmetric stretch vibration. It indicated that the Cu atoms did not bind with a carboxylic group, otherwise an absorption band at 1740cm−1 (CO stretch) should be observed. We also found the appearance of Cu–N (imidazole) stretching vibration mode at 780cm−1 and it suggested that l-histidine was connected to Cu atoms through Cu–N (imidazole) bonding (Fig. 3B). The above analysis demonstrates the successful introduction of l-histidine onto CuNPs [60,61].

The stability of as-prepared l-His-CuNPs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, after continuous irradiation of UV light (using hand-held ultraviolet analyzer, 365nm) for 60min, the fluorescence intensity of the l-His-CuNPs just had a little decrease, indicating that the l-His-CuNPs presented good anti-photobleaching property. As described in Fig. S2, different concentrations of NaCl had a negligible effect on the fluorescence of the l-His-CuNPs. Significantly, after three months, the morphology of the powder l-His-CuNPs did not change. When the solid samples were dissolved in ultrapure water, the fluorescence intensity of l-His-CuNPs solution did not change obviously (Fig. S3), suggesting the long-term storage stability and the possibility for commercial scale utilization.

It was found that the fluorescence intensity of l-His-CuNPs decreased markedly when Fe3+ was introduced (Fig. 4 A). Therefore, we considered that the synthesized l-His-CuNPs could be used for sensing Fe3+. The fluorescence and color change of l-His-CuNPs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ could be observed in the photograph of inset of Fig. 4A. Furthermore, in the presence of Fe3+, the well-dispersed l-His-CuNPs aggregate into large particles (Fig. 4B). Besides, we found that after adding ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) into the mixture solution (l-His-CuNPs with Fe3+), an intriguing fluorescence “turn off-on” phenomenon was observed (Fig. 4A), and the aggregates also restored to small particles (Fig. 4C).

In addition, as shown in Fig. 4D, the absorption spectra of Cu(NO3)2 (0.10M), l-His (0.10M), AA (0.10M), and l-His-CuNPs (5.4mgmL−1) in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were recorded, respectively. Only l-His-CuNPs exhibited a plasmonic absorption peak at 320nm and was decreased when Fe3+ existed. We speculated that Fe3+ bound to the nitrogen atom of an amine group on l-histidine, leading to the agglomeration of l-His-CuNPs, and finally, reducing the plasmonic inherence of the nanometer size regime [62].

At the beginning, the experiments of the concentration ratios of Cu2+/l-His, Cu2+/AA, and the reaction time were conducted to optimize the l-His-CuNPs probe. The optimal concentration ratios of Cu2+/l-His and Cu2+/AA are 9:2000 and 9:100, respectively, and the optimized synthesis time is 24h, as shown in Figs. S4, S5, and S6. The l-His-CuNPs synthesized under the above optimal conditions were used for the subsequent study.

Furthermore, several factors affecting the fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ have also been explored including the reaction time and the pH values. We explored the influence of incubation time on the response of l-His-CuNPs for Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity was decreased obviously within 30min when Fe3+ was introduced to l-His-CuNPs aqueous solution, as displayed in Fig. S7. And 60min later, the fluorescence intensity was almost decreased to the minimum, indicating that the reaction between Fe3+ and l-His-CuNPs was completed. Hence, the optimum incubation time was chosen as 60min. In addition, the fluorescence intensities of l-His-CuNPs were tested in BR buffer (pH 1.81–11.92) before and after adding Fe3+. Although the decrease of fluorescence intensity (F 0–F) of l-His-CuNPs/Fe3+ system was gradually enhanced with increasing acidity, the fluorescence of l-His-CuNPs/Fe3+ system was unstable under pH 1.81. Therefore, to obtain the high quenching efficiency and stable sensing condition, pH 4.10 was chosen as the optimum value (Fig. S8).

The quantitative detection of Fe3+ was conducted under the optimum experimental conditions by recording emission spectra of l-His-CuNPs after addition of different concentrations of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 5 A. The decrease in fluorescence intensity of l-His-CuNPs was proportional to the concentration of Fe3+ from 0.10–20μM. And the regression equation was: F 0 − F =41.831C +13.937 (R2 =0.998), where C presents the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection for Fe3+ was 82nM based on 3σ/slope (where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank measures), which is lower than other different fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ [51,54,55,63–68] (Table 1 ). The results revealed that this probe has the capacity for detecting trace Fe3+ and exhibits a superior sensitivity.

To ensure that Fe3+ can be recognized from potential interferents, 100μM Na+, K+, Li+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Al3+, and Fe2+ were added to the l-His-CuNPs solution, and the fluorescence responses were investigated (Fig. 6 A). In addition, as some anions may coexist with Fe3+ in natural water, 10 kinds of common anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, Ac−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, and PO4 3−, 100μM for each) were tested to explore their influences on the determination of Fe3+ (Fig. 6B). We found that an obvious decrease of the fluorescence intensity only occurred in the l-His-CuNPs/Fe3+ system. Therefore, l-His-CuNPs have a good selectivity for Fe3+ detection.

As a paramagnetic transition metal ion with empty d shells, Fe3+ has the propensity to quench the fluorescence of the neighboring fluorophore by energy or electron transfer [69]. Besides, Fe3+ has a higher thermodynamic affinity and can chelate with the nitrogen atom of amine group very fast [70]. Due to the binding force of Fe3+ to the nitrogen atom, the neighboring l-His-CuNPs could be pulled closely. Thus, we speculated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the assay was aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) effect. The TEM characterization was investigated to testify the assumptive fluorescence quenching mechanism. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the well-dispersed l-His-CuNPs showed aggregated morphology when a certain amount of Fe3+ was added to the l-His-CuNPs solution.

Furthermore, in order to make full sense of the fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence recovery experiments were conducted. As is known to us all, EDTA is the most commonly used chelate because of its strong chelating ability to different metal ions, particularly to Fe3+ [71]. The method of using EDTA to chelate Fe3+ from other nanomaterials such as Au nanoclusters has been reported [72]. Here, after adding EDTA to the l-His-CuNPs/Fe3+ system, the chelating ability between Fe3+ and EDTA is so strong that the EDTA can compete with l-His-CuNPs for Fe3+, resulting in the formation of stable chelate complexes of Fe3+-EDTA. As the interaction between Fe3+ and the nitrogen atom of amine group in l-histidine is broken, the fluorescence “turn off-on” phenomenon was observed (Fig. 4A). Furthermore, as displayed in Fig. 4C, the TEM image shows that the l-His-CuNPs aggregates were restored to small particles after adding EDTA to the mixture (l-His-CuNPs with Fe3+), further proving the reason of the fluorescence change.

To investigate the practicality of this method, we applied the l-His-CuNPs sensing system to analyze Fe3+ in real-water-samples (tap water and Jialing River water). First, 10μL of sample solutions after pretreatment was mixed with 100μL of 5.4mgmL−1 l-His-CuNPs solution. Then the above mixture was diluted to 500μL using BR buffer (10mM, pH 4.10). Furthermore, the accuracy of this method for Fe3+ sensing in real-water-samples was further evaluated with recovery experiments. Sample solutions were spiked with different concentrations of standard Fe3+ solution. The above solution (10μL) was mixed with 100μL of 5.4mgmL−1 l-His-CuNPs solution, and the mixture was diluted to 500μL using BR buffer (10mM, pH 4.10). Finally, the fluorescence intensities were measured after incubated at 25°C for 60min. As listed in Table S1, the recoveries of Fe3+ in tap water samples and Jialing River samples were in the acceptable range of 82.8%–107.4%. Furthermore, the result of this proposed method has a good agreement with that obtained by flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS). The above results suggested the detection of Fe3+ by l-His-CuNPs probe was reliable and accurate in natural water.

In summary, a simple and one-pot method was developed to synthesize fluorescent l-His-CuNPs using l-histidine as the stabilizing agent and AA as the reducing agent under ambient temperature (25°C) without stirring. The water-soluble l-His-CuNP probes were employed to sensitively and selectively detect Fe3+ with the LOD of 82nM, linearity from 0.10 to 20μM. Furthermore, the l-His-CuNPs were successfully applied for detecting Fe3+ in tap water and Jialing River water samples, suggesting that this fluorescent probe has good practicability for the detection of real samples. The novel synthetic strategy is promising to detect Fe3+ in food, medicine, waste water or other fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131, 21273174) and the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001, CSTC-2013jjB00002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.06.079.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step green synthetic approach for the preparation of multicolor emitting copper nanoclusters and their applications in chemical species sensing and bioimaging
Fluorescent copper nanoparticles: recent advances in synthesis and applications for sensing metal ions

Solvent-dependent surface plasmon response and oxidation of copper nanocrystals

Synthesis and antimicrobial activity of copper nanoparticles

A novel fluorescence and naked eye sensor for iodide in urine basedon the iodide induced oxidative etching and aggregation of Cu nanoclusters
Förster resonance-energy-transfer detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol using copper nanoclusters
Protein-directed synthesis of pH-responsive red fluorescent copper nanoclusters and applications in cellular imaging and catalysts
Selective dsDNA-templated formation of copper nanoparticles in solution
Ultra-small fluorescent metal nanoclusters: synthesis and biological applications
Water-soluble and lowly toxic sulphur quantum dots
Two to tango: regulation of mammalian iron metabolism
Surface catalyzed oxidative oligomerization of 17β-estradiol by Fe3+-saturated montmorillonite
Removal of iron from potable water using a trickling filter
Physico-chemical and biological iron removal from potable water
Iron ion removal from groundwater using banana ash, carbonates and bicarbonates of Na and K, and their mixtures
Isotopic analysis of the metabolically relevant transition metals Cu, Fe and Zn in human blood from vegetarians and omnivores using multi-collector ICP-mass spectrometry
An iron stable isotope comparison between human erythrocytes and plasma
Aldazine-based colorimetric sensors for Cu2+ and Fe3+
Effects of iron overload and hepatitis C virus positivity in determining progression of liver fibrosis in thalassemia following bone marrow transplantation
The influence of menstrual blood loss and age on the isotopic composition of Cu, Fe and Zn in human whole blood
A novel rhodamine-based fluorescent and colorimetric off–on chemosensor and investigation of the recognizing behavior towards Fe3+
Iron (III) selective molecular and supramolecular fluorescent probes
Erythrocyte membrane fatty acid composition is related to overloaded plasma ferritin in Chinese males with angiographic coronary artery disease
Oxidative damage to rat brain in iron and copper overloads
Coordination chemistry and biology of chelators for the treatment of iron overload disorders
Fe isotope variations in natural materials measured using high mass resolution multiple collector ICPMS
A novel method for the filterless preconcentration of iron
Determination of Cu, FeMn and Zn in blood fractions by SEC-HPLC-ICP-AES coupling
Colorimetric detection of Fe(III) ions using label-free gold nanoparticles and acidic thiourea mixture
Chemical speciation of iron in seawater by cathodic Stripping voltammetry with dihydroxynaphthalene
Study on a highly selective fluorescent chemosensor for Fe3+ based on 1,3,4-oxadiazole and phosphonic acid
One-step synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent copper nanoparticles for label-free detection of manganese ions
Highly stable antibacterial silver nanoparticles as selective fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ions
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron (III) ions and dopamine
Three rhodamine-based off–on chemosensors with high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ imaging in living cells
A novel ratiometric fluorescent Fe3+ sensor based on a phenanthroimidazole chromophore
CHEF induced highly selective and sensitive turn-on fluorogenic and colorimetric sensor for Fe3+
Colorimetric detection of Fe3+ ions using pyrophosphate functionalized gold nanoparticles
Lysozyme-directed synthesis of platinum nanoclusters as a mimic oxidase
Speciation of gold(III)–L-histidine complex: a multi-instrumental approach
Raman and IR study on copper binding of histamine
Surface plasmon tunability and emission sensitivity of ultrasmall fluorescent copper nanoclusters
DOPA-mediated reduction allows the facile synthesis of fluorescent gold nanoclusters for use as sensing probes for ferric ions
A novel colorimetric and turn-on fluorescent chemosensor for iron(III) ion detection and its application to cellular imaging
One-step blue-green synthesis of fluorescent bimetallic Au/Ag nanoclusters for temperature sensing and in vitro detection of Fe3+
Highly selective ratiometric fluorescent detection of Fe3+ with a polyphenyl derivative
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped and amino acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots from glycine, and their application to the fluorometric determination of ferric ion
A unique turn-on fluorescence signalling strategy for highly specific detection of ascorbic acid using carbon dots as sensing probe
Reactions of Metal Chelates in Soils and Nutrient Solutions, Micronutrients in Agriculture
Multiplex sensor for detection of different metal ions based on on–off of fluorescent gold nanoclusters

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shu Min Lin is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Shuo Geng is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Na Li is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Shi Gang Liu is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His major research interest is spectrum analysis.

Nian Bing Li is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and PhD degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Korea. His research interests are the developments of electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.

Hong Qun Luo is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a visiting scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensor.
